moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Hungarian Opening, a quiet but flexible approach that avoids the sharpness of 1.e4 or 1.d4. This move prepares to fianchetto our bishop on g2, where it will control the important long diagonal (a1-h8) and support central play. The beauty of 1.g3 is that it keeps options open—we haven't committed to a specific pawn structure, so we can adapt to whatever Black plays while maintaining a solid, space-conscious setup.

Black mirrors our fianchetto plan with 1...g6, creating the Symmetrical Variation where both sides prepare to develop bishops to g2. This move is flexible and solid, controlling the long diagonal and keeping the center fluid. By matching our setup, Black avoids allowing us an advantage from our unusual opening choice, though the resulting positions tend to favor whoever understands the nuances of fianchettoed structures better—making preparation and understanding pawn breaks (like c4 or d4) crucial in the middlegame.
